summary.intent: Review all security issues on the dorian-pepperberry branch and fix them, culminating in a design that restricts privileged channel commands (/approve, /deny) to users explicitly on the DM allowlist.
summary.outcome: All four security issues were fixed and pushed: /attach scoped to same-account sessions, /approve and /deny restricted to allowlisted senders (checked centrally in the gateway), approval notifications truncated to 80 chars, and MS Teams updated to use resolve_model/resolve_agent_id. 1762 tests passing.

repo: The DM allowlist (channel account config's allowlist()) is the single source of truth for who is an authorized operator — Moltis is a personal assistant, not a multi-user bot, so allowlist = owner.
ChannelRegistry.account_config(account_id) returns Option<Box<dyn ChannelConfigView>> which exposes allowlist() for sender authorization checks.
session_metadata.list_account_sessions(channel_type, account_id) scopes session listing to the same channel+account; the broader list() was the root cause of the /attach hijacking risk.
gating::is_allowed() returns true for empty allowlists (open policy), so approval auth needs an explicit non-empty allowlist check rather than reusing is_allowed().
All channels except MS Teams were already using resolve_model/resolve_agent_id via ChannelConfigView; MS Teams was hardcoding model and None for agent_id.
Slack DM channels start with 'D', making channel_id.starts_with('D') a valid but heuristic DM detection.
CUDA crate excluded from clippy/build on macOS — this is expected per CLAUDE.md, not a real error.
dispatch_command trait is defined in crates/channels/src/plugin.rs and implemented in 6+ channel crates plus a gateway implementation; changes to it require touching 11+ files.

code.finding: /attach command had no authorization check — used session_metadata.list() instead of list_account_sessions(), exposing all sessions to any channel user
/approve and /deny had no sender authorization; fix added is_sender_on_allowlist() helper checking sender_id against channel account's DM allowlist via channel_registry
Approval notifications broadcast full command string to channel; fixed with truncate_command_preview() capped at 80 chars using Cow<'_, str>
MS Teams hardcoded agent_id: None instead of config.resolve_agent_id(); also used config.model.clone() instead of config.resolve_model()
dispatch_command trait is the extension point for all channel command handling; authorization should NOT be added here as a parameter — centralize in the gateway instead

workflow: Centralizing authorization in the gateway (not the trait) keeps channel implementations simple and prevents future channels from needing to reason about authorization logic.

The SenderAuth enum approach was tried and reverted — encoding auth context as a trait parameter pushes policy decisions to each channel implementor, which is error-prone and was the wrong abstraction level.
Using Cow<'_, str> for string helpers that may or may not allocate matches project idioms (noted during approval truncation).
Running cargo fmt and cargo clippy after all edits before committing is part of the project workflow.
Subagents were used to parallelize reverting SenderAuth from many call sites, which worked but required verifying compilation after.

friction: The SenderAuth enum was added, then the design evolved and it had to be fully reverted across 11 files — significant churn from premature implementation before the design was settled.
Multiple rounds of design iteration on the authorization model (DM-only → SenderAuth enum → allowlist-based) required repeated large refactors.
Telegram has many dispatch_command call sites spread across handle_message_direct and handle_callback_query, requiring many individual edits.

Determining DM context per-channel required different heuristics for each: Slack uses channel_id prefix, Discord uses guild_id.is_none(), Telegram uses ChatKind::Private, Matrix uses ChatType enum — no unified abstraction existed.
A test for truncate_command_preview failed on first run because the secret was within the 80-char limit — required adjusting test data.
open_items: Channel command allowlist config (per-channel config field specifying which commands non-allowlisted users can run from group chats) was explicitly deferred as a follow-up issue — touches config schema, validation, templates, and docs.
Approval/deny commands could potentially be scoped to the session creator rather than (or in addition to) the allowlist, for finer-grained control in multi-user scenarios.
The Slack DM detection heuristic (channel_id.starts_with('D')) is fragile — a proper channel_type field from Slack's API would be more reliable.
MS Teams DM detection uses !is_group which should be verified against all MS Teams conversation types (personal, groupChat, channel).

Empty allowlist behavior for approval auth (deny all) differs from empty allowlist behavior for chat gating (allow all) — this asymmetry could be surprising and may need documentation or a dedicated config flag.
